segs.utf8:
Transcriber: Anvesh Boy
Reviewer: Emma Gon
As a six year old girl,

being born in Hungary
 behind the Iron Curtain,

I loved watching the snow
 covered mountains of the Alps,
just beyond the border.

The border which we were
not allowed to cross.

One mountain, especially
Schneeberg, Snow Mountain,

was covered with snow
 all year round. It was magical.
Even on the hottest summer days,
the snow was shining on its peak.

Only after the Iron Curtain fell
and I was an adult,
I could travel to Schneeberg,

the mountain I had longed
 to hike for so long.
And for my greatest surprise,

I discovered that the snow
in the summer was not snow at all.
It was shining white stone on its peak.
If we observe things from a distance,
we can have an image of the truth,

which might be completely
far away from reality.

But if we get close,
we discover the truth, the reality,
maybe something completely different.

As a documentary photographer, my goal
is to bring this truth closer to people.

I travel to the furthest
places of the planet,

to the Arctic and Antarctica
to document scientific expeditions.

Not everyone can
 journey to those places,

but to understand
 its reality is essential

to understand 
what is happening in the planet.

Exactly ten years ago, in 2015, the year
when the Paris Agreement was signed,

I traveled for 
my very first polar expedition,
and since then I documented 27,

including one place
which is so dear to my heart,

Ny-Ålesund Research Station in Svalbard,
the Norwegian Arctic.

I traveled there as a photographer
for the Alfred Wegener Institute,
and I’m hosted there

by the French German 
Arctic Research Base AWIPEV.

Svalbard is 
the epicenter of global warming.
Since 1993, winter average temperature
has risen here by 6 to 8 Celsius.
We know this because scientists

of Alfred Wegener Institute
conduct atmospheric research,

launching a weather balloon
 every single day, even today.
We can understand climate change only
if we look at long terms of observations.

If we look at the data
of the dedicated people,

who are collecting
vital data for humanity.

These are the stories
I’m interested about.

I want to bring the scientists into light,
who often works in shadows.

Ny-Ålesund Research Station is
the northernmost community of the planet.

In 1963, it was a mining town
 almost for 50 years.
In 1963, it turned into a scientific hub

under the leadership of Norway
with the Norwegian Polar Institute

and Kings Bay Climate and
 environmental research is happening here.

Scientists, engineers and 
support staff work and live here,

far away from 
their families and friends.
No civilians live here.

They are right there where
the changes are happening close to that,

and I see myself as a messenger
bringing their findings to people.

They live there
between one and a half year,
up to ten years in this remote place.

They live and work
in the land of polar bear,
where safety is key to their survival.

They have to do polar bear
safety training regularly.

On the snow covered
 glaciers and slopes,
avalanche can be triggered very quickly.

They train together because
in a case of an emergency,
they have to count on each other.

We all know that the Arctic is the fastest
changing environment of our planet,

but who are the scientists
behind the data?
I want to show their faces.

Hygee, changing air filters
at Zeppelin Observatory

Arndt changing the precipitation bucket
for meteorological observation.

This is one of the longest
running observations

run by the Norwegian
 Polar Institute since 1967.

Lola collects algae for sea urchins
 living in the marine lab.

Fika transfers the data
from the permafrost station.

Alex, who is getting ready for her dive
in minus one Celsius degree water
to collect samples from the sea floor.
Forty people live in this town

and making sure that long term
observations are running

even in the polar night,
which lasts for four months.

Being far away from their loved ones,
social life is very important.

Getting together
 after long working days

like going out in small huts
for painting.

There are four legged Friends
in town as well,

and they are very much loved and 
taken care by the entire community.

Dogs in remote places are very important
 for wellbeing and mental health.

I’m so happy to be a photographer,
because many events I can skip
and having an excuse that I’m so sorry.

But I will not do this
 because I have to photograph it
and one of them is the ice bathing.
I think I will never, ever do that.

On that day it was -29
and the water was -1.8 Celsius.

In the Arctic, the average temperature
 is rising 3 to 4 times faster.

In Svalbard, this is seven times faster
in a place with no industry

and only 3000 inhabitants
on the entire archipelago.

Infrastructure here
 is built on permafrost,
the frozen ground which is thawing,

houses become unstable
and requiring new foundations.
This is visible in the entire Arctic,

and entire indigenous
communities have to be relocated.

Ny-Ålesund and Svalbard doesn’t
have an indigenous community.
It is an outpost for scientists

observing the fastest changing
 environment of our planet.

It is a hub for polar exploration,
a gateway to the North Pole.

Roald Amundsen, 
the Norwegian explorer,

started his expedition
99 years ago with his team

and with the Norge airship
flying over the North Pole from here.

The mast of the airship
is still standing there,

and the red light leads
scientists back into town
during the dark winter month.
From this era of the exploration,

we don’t know [how] many if any,
[were] female scientists or explorers.

Today I work with 
incredible female scientists
and I want to highlight their work
like Susannah, Julia,
Ioana,
Yulia,
Ingrid,
Fika,
Charlotte,
Zigna Maria,
Verena,
Apolline.
The life follows here.

The rhythm of the calendar
to maintain a sense of home.

Locals are celebrating
 festivals and holiday.

On special events,
you have to wear a costume,

which you can borrow 
from the local costume storage.

You would definitely need one,
if you are the angel
during the Nordic Santa Lucia Festival.

On special occasions, dinner 
is served in the Amundsen villa,

where Roald Amundsen stayed
during his expeditions.

But the most touching
 event of all is the memorial
for the 21 men who died here
during the last mining accident in 1962,

after which the town transformed
into a scientific hub.

Locals are walking
 in quietness with lamps
to the monument remembering them.

Ny-Ålesund is located in Kongsfjorden,
the fjord which used to be frozen,

and scientists and residents
could cross it by skis.

This is not happening
for the past ten years.

Locals can cross
 the fjord only by boat.

Warmer waters 
and warmer air temperature
prevent the fjord from freezing.

Warmer waters also bring
invasive species with it.

In 2022, biologists of 
Alfred Wegener Institute

discovered helmet jellyfish
for the very first time.

An invasive species
 from warmer waters,

which feeds faster,
reproduces faster

and destroys the slower paced 
ecosystem here.

The goal of my work is to bring
the truth closer to people,

even if it’s far away 
that you don’t think

there is snow on the top
of Schneeberg all year round.

That we know the truth and
 we can take well-informed decisions

for ourselves, for humanity
and for the future of the planet.
Thank you so much.
(Applause)
